perturbation.original_text: FIIOC may terminate this Agreement at any time upon ninety (90) days' written notice to Company.
perturbation.changed_text: FIIOC may terminate this Agreement at any time upon thirty (30) days' written notice to Company.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 30 days could be problematic if the company needs a longer period to transition services or if state laws require longer notice periods for contracts of this nature.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Chapter 93A doesn't explicitly mandate notice periods for contract termination, unreasonably short notice periods (30 days) can be viewed as unfair or deceptive practices, especially if the termination significantly harms one party. Courts may consider industry standards and the affected party's ability to mitigate damages when determining if the notice was reasonable. Therefore, drastically shortening the notice period can be a violation of this law if deemed unfair in the context of the relationship between the parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for contract termination from 90 to 30 days. The Law Explanation suggests that such a short notice period could be viewed as an unfair practice under Chapter 93A if it significantly harms one party. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state that "Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful" under Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93a, Section 2. Therefore, reducing the notice period could contradict this law if deemed unfair, making the contract inconsistent and potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, compensation for each calendar quarter will not exceed [ ].
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, compensation for each calendar quarter will not exceed $100.00.
perturbation.explanation: Capping compensation at a nominal amount such as $100 per quarter could violate minimum wage laws if Company's services effectively constitute employment and the implied hourly rate falls below the state minimum wage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Minimum Wage Law, M.G.L. c. 151, § 1
perturbation.law_explanation: If the quarterly compensation of $100 for the services provided by New York Life Insurance and Annuity Corporation falls below the state's minimum wage standards when considering the time spent on the described duties, this directly contradicts M.G.L. c. 151, § 1. This statute mandates a minimum hourly wage for employees, and if this agreement effectively creates an employer-employee relationship with compensation beneath the minimum wage floor, it's in violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 151, Section 1 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 151 MINIMUM FAIR WAGES Section 1 Oppressive and unreasonable wages; validity of contracts Section 1 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1: Oppressive and unreasonable wages; validity of contracts Section 1. It is hereby declared to be against public policy for any employer to employ any person in an occupation in this commonwealth at an oppressive and unreasonable wage as defined in section two, and any contract, agreement or understanding for or in relation to such employment shall be null and void. A wage of less than $15.00 per hour, in any occupation, as defined in this chapter, shall conclusively be presumed to be oppressive and unreasonable, wherever the term ''minimum wage'' is used in this chapter, unless the commissioner has expressly approved or shall expressly approve the establishment and payment of a lesser wage under the provisions of sections seven and nine. Notwithstanding the provisions of this section, in no case shall the minimum wage rate be less than $.50 higher than the effective federal minimum rate. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The changed text, which caps compensation at $100 per quarter, contradicts the minimum wage law outlined in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The snippet states that 'A wage of less than $15.00 per hour, in any occupation... shall conclusively be presumed to be oppressive and unreasonable.' If the services provided by New York Life Insurance and Annuity Corporation constitute employment and the time spent on those duties results in an hourly rate less than $15.00, the capped compensation violates this minimum wage requirement, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and the provisions hereof interpreted under and in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed and the provisions hereof interpreted under and in accordance with the laws of the Vatican City.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing the law of Vatican City, a jurisdiction with a legal system vastly different from that of Massachusetts and the United States, and lacking readily accessible commercial law, can render the contract unenforceable due to issues of public policy and conflict of laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Comity and Public Policy under Massachusetts Law
perturbation.law_citation: N/A (Principles of Comity and Public Policy)
perturbation.law_explanation: While there isn't a specific statute, Massachusetts courts recognize the principle of comity, which generally respects the laws and judicial decisions of other jurisdictions. However, this respect is limited by public policy. Selecting Vatican City law, which lacks established commercial law similar to Massachusetts, could be seen as contrary to public policy because it could undermine the predictability and fairness of contractual relationships. This choice introduces uncertainty and could lead a Massachusetts court to refuse to enforce the choice-of-law provision if it significantly offends the state's public policy or established legal norms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: comity | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you comity Comity refers to courts of one state or jurisdiction respecting the laws and judicial decisions of other jurisdictions – whether state, federal or international – not as a matter of obligation but out of deference and mutual respect. In Constitutional Law, the enforcement of judgments between states in the United States is governed by the Comity Clause of the Constitution - Article IV, § 2, Clause 2 . The first-filed rule, for instance, is a rule of comity for resolving conflicts of jurisdictions where parallel actions are filed in different federal district courts . If two or more substantially identical actions are proceeding in different courts, the court with the action that was filed later should defer to the jurisdiction of the court with the action that was filed first by dismissing , staying , or transferring the later-filed suit. See also: comity of nations [Last reviewed in January of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords lawsuits-court Wex CIVICS local governmental law multi-jurisdictional law conflicts of law the Constitution THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal procedure wex definitions civil procedure constitutional law courts and procedure foreign perspective government international law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces Massachusetts law with the law of Vatican City. The Perturbation Explanation notes that the Vatican City legal system lacks readily accessible commercial law, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable. The Law Explanation adds that selecting Vatican City law could be seen as contrary to public policy because it could undermine the predictability and fairness of contractual relationships. The scraped law snippet defines 'comity' as courts respecting the laws of other jurisdictions, but this respect is limited by public policy concerns, suggesting that choosing Vatican City law might not be respected if it offends Massachusetts' public policy or established legal norms.
